speaker: interviewee
text: This is to show you the, the route I took when I tried to escape from this Schouwburg, or the theater. Here is the creche. And here is the, the Schouwburg. This is the side entrance where I left later on to-- where I escaped in-- just by walking away. If I had known that, I could have saved myself a fair amount of difficulties. Anyhow, I went-- from the creche, I went over to the Schouwburg. And then my father said, well, just run when you get to this little safe haven here. So I went from here. I walked over here to go across here. And at this point, I started running away over to this direction here. Further down-- no, no, not really, can't show this-- but further down here, that's when I collapsed and fell to the ground and was picked up by the German soldier, who then dragged me back over to creche here. And then I-- there was a side entrance here and another exit here that led me back to-- this is the sidewalk here-- that led me back to the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: --go. Stand by. We're recording.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the, the farmhouse in Achttienhoven. This is the entrance here. The haystack was in front of the house. This is the stairway that leads up to the attic. This is the larder-- I was standing in front. And somebody was looking out for me-- and that I felt they were watching me. Going through here, there's a table here. This was the dining room here. Going through the, the important room where nobody ever sat, there was this little room here. And here was the-- the-- the bed with the doors. And here was the little stand with-- how do you call that-- with the medicines on it and, and was a door here. Just giving you the layout of the-- of the land, you might say.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, we're rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is just a photograph of myself. And I was, I think, in second class of the-- of high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I imagine this was 1949 or '50.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you mention your name one more time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stan Rubens, in my younger years. One second, one second.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Stan Rubens and-- when I was working in the Surfers Paradise as a musician. It was a, a group called-- we called ourselves The Flamingo Trio. We played a lot of Latin American music. I was about, I think, 23 or 24 years old at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, we're rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm very proud of my daughter, Ronny Rubens. There's a photograph of her. She is trying to [LAUGHS] become a paid actress in-- in Hollywood. She lives in Burbank at the moment. Just give me a sign.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stand by. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister, Ronny-- Ronny Rubens nee, but Hemelraad now. 'Hemelraad' is spelled H-E-M-E-L-R-A-A-D. At present, she is a widow. Her husband passed away some, some years-- three years ago. She lives in Sydney, Australia. Also interesting that she moved away pretty far from Holland, even though she likes to go back to Holland very much. My sister, Ronny Rubens.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Mrs. Dien van Amerongen, or as I call her, Tante Dien-- where I was underground for about 10 months, and who was very, very beautiful and loving towards me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken two years ago, upon the occasion when she was given an-- citation by the Israeli Consulate about her work that she had done during the war by hiding my, my cousin and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is how I wanted to prove my independence, with my own boat called with-- which I sailed-- it took 18 days from Los Angeles or Newport Beach to Kane'ohe Bay in Honolulu, Hawaii. Give me a count. 'As sure as the snake will capture its prey, fate has already designed a juxtaposition of our lives intertwined in the darkness already aligned-- a speck of dust, infinitesimal, small, presenting the lives in the sphere, where Earth hurdles on through space on its way to make your spirit unclear.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you rolling? OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SINGING] Strange day today-- strange the way we feel. Strange the way that people talk. Strange I don't get wet in the rain.

speaker: interviewee
text: Strange day today-- I was sitting all alone, and no one came along. But am I different than the day before? Strange visions that I cannot explain-- tell me, what am I thinking? What am I fighting in vain?

speaker: interviewee
text: Strange day today, though not as strange as it seems. Thank heaven, awake again from those bad, strange, very-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --strange dreams. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: I should have unplugged--

speaker: interviewee
text: [SINGING] Strange day today-- strange the way we feel. Strange the way that people talk. Strange I don't get wet in the rain.

speaker: interviewee
text: Strange day today-- I was sitting all alone, and no one came along. But am I different than the day before? Strange visions that I cannot explain. Tell me, what am I thinking? What am I fighting in vain?

speaker: interviewee
text: Strange day today-- no, not as strange as it seems. Thank heaven, awake again from those bad, strange, very strange dreams.
